$comment: The destination-facts rules both languages compute on read, agreed between them. The service derives these in AppService::get_trip from a stored snapshot; mock.ts derives them again in its own getTrip. Nothing compared the two before this file. voyalier-core's parity_trip_facts_matches_the_contract holds Rust to it; apps/web's parity.test.ts holds the mock.
$scope: Covers the rule-bearing derived fields only. Astro is deliberately absent: the mock's sun/moon times are documented as fictional fixture values, so pinning them would either fail on sight or force ephemeris maths into TypeScript. Nearest airports and world heritage are absent too — canned fixtures whose values the core's own unit tests already pin, with no read-time rule worth agreeing on.

timeDifference.$why: Two stored UTC offsets in, one wall-clock gap out. Minutes rather than hours because sub-hour zones are real, and zero is a genuine answer (same time as home) rather than 'unknown'.

cases.name: same wall clock is a real answer, not a missing one
sub-hour zones stay exact
cases.$why: India is +330 and Nepal +345. Rounding either to hours loses the 15 minutes that make them different.

holidaysWithin.$why: The snapshot stores every holiday across the trip's years so a date edit re-narrows without re-fetching. Narrowing is therefore a read-time rule, and it does three things rather than one: filter to the window inclusive at both ends, sort by date then name, and collapse exact duplicates. The sort and the dedup are the two a mirror forgets — the mock did, until this file.

cases.name: inclusive at both ends, excluding the days either side
sorts by date, then by name within a date
cases.$why: The source returns years in fetch order, and two holidays can share a date. Without the sort the panel lists them in whatever order the feed happened to use.

cases.name: collapses exact duplicates
cases.$why: Overlapping per-year fetches can return the same holiday twice. Same date and same name is one holiday, however many times the feed said it.

cases.name: no holidays in the window is empty, not absent

tipping.$why: Bundled, resolved fresh from the destination's country code on every read so a corrected guide never freezes into a stored snapshot. The mock hardcodes the fixture country's line; this holds that line to the core's table, and holds both to returning nothing rather than inventing guidance for an uncurated country.

cases.expected: Not customary and can cause confusion — service is already included.

cases.$why: Not a curated country. No guidance is the honest answer; a generic one would be Voyalier asserting a custom it has no source for.

countryFacts.$why: Also bundled and resolved fresh from the country code. The mock carries only the fixture's Japan; this holds those values to the core's table so mock mode cannot show the UI a different Japan than the service would.
